page.content: The issue of organ donation remains a complex and multifaceted topic, often influenced by personal beliefs, cultural contexts, and social perceptions. In examining the attitudes towards organ donation within different faculties, the data presented in the accompanying table reveals significant insights into the reasons for not donating organs. Specifically, the column labeled "Reasons for not donating organ" highlights a variety of personal and religious beliefs that contribute to this reluctance. For instance, the data extracts indicate that some individuals perceive organ donation as inappropriate from a religious standpoint, a sentiment echoed by 29 respondents (16.3%) from the medical faculty and 12 respondents (12.8%) from the law faculty. These figures suggest a notable divergence in perspectives based on academic background, which may reflect broader societal influences or educational experiences. Additionally, the subthemes presented in the table illustrate the social activities and personal narratives that shape individuals' understanding and acceptance of organ donation. A more nuanced understanding of these factors is essential for developing targeted educational interventions that can address misconceptions and foster a more favorable view towards organ donation across diverse populations.

lines.text: The issue of organ donation remains a complex and multifaceted topic, often influenced by personal beliefs, cultural contexts,
and social perceptions. In examining the attitudes towards organ donation within different faculties, the data presented in the
accompanying table reveals significant insights into the reasons for not donating organs. Specifically, the column labeled
"Reasons for not donating organ" highlights a variety of personal and religious beliefs that contribute to this reluctance. For
instance, the data extracts indicate that some individuals perceive organ donation as inappropriate from a religious standpoint, a
sentiment echoed by 29 respondents (16.3%) from the medical faculty and 12 respondents (12.8%) from the law faculty. These
figures suggest a notable divergence in perspectives based on academic background, which may reflect broader societal
influences or educational experiences. Additionally, the subthemes presented in the table illustrate the social activities and
personal narratives that shape individuals' understanding and acceptance of organ donation. A more nuanced understanding of
these factors is essential for developing targeted educational interventions that can address misconceptions and foster a more
favorable view towards organ donation across diverse populations.

page.html:
SubthemesData extracts
1.1 Social activities‘I was once sat by the pool and I heard this woman say, “Look at that woman's feet,” and I felt absolutely dreadful.’ [P1: using topical therapy]
‘So it [topical therapy] practically destroys any chance of having a social life or anything like that.’ [P5: prescribed topical therapy but using tanning beds]
‘... I'll plan it [topical therapy] if I know we will be going out or if we are going out for a family meal or whatever. I'll plan to use stuff so when we do go out, it's not noticeable that I've used anything.’ [P15: using topical therapy and photochemotherapy]
1.2 Exercise and physical activity‘I feel it's quite difficult to do activities like swimming or anything which exposes too much of my arms and legs really.’ [P4: using topical therapy]
‘You're meant to use it [topical therapy] twice a day, which is not practical ... it cuts into the time that I would like to go for a jog ... ’ [P5: prescribed topical therapy but using tanning beds]
1.3 Work commitments‘But actually, you know, if you've got a job, even if it's just a 9 to 5 job; to go to your UVB 3 times a week for a 12‐week period, that's quite a lot. Let alone if you travel a lot. It then becomes unsustainable.’ [P6: using topical therapy, previously used phototherapy]
‘You just can't do it [follow the topical therapy regimen]. Especially working and things like that, you just can't do it.’ [P2: using topical therapy]

Data extracts
1.1 Social activities
‘I was once sat by the pool and I heard this woman say, “Look at that woman's feet,” and I felt absolutely dreadful.’ [P1: using topical therapy]
‘So it [topical therapy] practically destroys any chance of having a social life or anything like that.’ [P5: prescribed topical therapy but using tanning beds]

‘... I'll plan it [topical therapy] if I know we will be going out or if we are going out for a family meal or whatever. I'll plan to use stuff so when we do go out, it's not noticeable that I've used anything.’ [P15: using topical therapy and photochemotherapy]
1.2 Exercise and physical activity
‘I feel it's quite difficult to do activities like swimming or anything which exposes too much of my arms and legs really.’ [P4: using topical therapy]
‘You're meant to use it [topical therapy] twice a day, which is not practical ... it cuts into the time that I would like to go for a jog ... ’ [P5: prescribed topical therapy but using tanning beds]
1.3 Work commitments

‘But actually, you know, if you've got a job, even if it's just a 9 to 5 job; to go to your UVB 3 times a week for a 12‐week period, that's quite a lot. Let alone if you travel a lot. It then becomes unsustainable.’ [P6: using topical therapy, previously used phototherapy]
‘You just can't do it [follow the topical therapy regimen]. Especially working and things like that, you just can't do it.’ [P2: using topical therapy]

Reasons for not donating organMedical faculty(n=356) n (%)Law faculty(n=142) n (%)
Organ donation is not appropriate religiously29 (16.3)12 (12.8)
Afraid of taking organs before the death42 (23.3)29 (30.9)
Does not want disruption of body integrity after the death37 (20.6)19 (20.2)
Fear that their organs will be given to those who do not want12 (6.7)4 (4.3)
Fear of commercial exploitation of organs26 (14.4)15 (16.0)
Others34 (18.9)15 (16.0)
Total180 (100.0)94 (100.0)
